1.title: Ben Hogan wins the Open Championship at Carnoustie
1.text: Ben Hogan won the Open Championship at Carnoustie in his only appearance in the event, completing the 'Hogan Slam' of three majors in 1953. He shot a closing 68 to win by four strokes.

1.title: Arnold Palmer triumphs at Royal Birkdale
1.text: Arnold Palmer won the Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, the first of his two Claret Jugs, prevailing in difficult weather to beat Dai Rees by one stroke.

1.title: Henry Cotton's record-setting Open round
1.text: Henry Cotton's 1934 Open Championship campaign at Royal St George's featured a second-round 65 so celebrated that it gave its name to the Dunlop '65' golf ball.

1.title: Tony Jacklin ends Britain's Open drought
1.text: Tony Jacklin would win the 1969 Open Championship at Royal Lytham, becoming the first Englishman in 18 years to lift the Claret Jug before adding the 1970 U.S. Open.

1.title: Walter Hagen wins third Open Championship
1.text: Walter Hagen captured the Open Championship at Royal St George's in 1928, one of his four Claret Jugs, reinforcing his standing as the dominant professional of the era.

1.title: Nick Faldo opens with low rounds at St Andrews
1.text: Nick Faldo began his dominant run at the 1990 Open Championship at St Andrews, a week in which he would win by five strokes for his second Claret Jug in three years.

2.title: Walter Hagen becomes first American-born Open champion
2.text: Walter Hagen won the Open Championship at Royal St George's, becoming the first American-born golfer to capture the Claret Jug, beating George Duncan and Jim Barnes by one.

2.title: Bobby Jones wins his first Open Championship
2.text: Bobby Jones won the Open Championship at Royal Lytham & St Annes, the first of his three Claret Jugs, helped by a famous bunker shot on the 17th hole now marked by a plaque.

2.title: Jack White wins the Open at Sandwich
2.text: Jack White won the 1904 Open Championship at Royal St George's, posting 296 to become the first champion to break 300 over 72 holes in the event.

2.title: Lee Trevino's hot summer of 1971
2.text: In the summer of 1971 Lee Trevino was assembling one of golf's great runs, winning the U.S. Open, Canadian Open and Open Championship within a matter of weeks.

2.title: Henry Cotton wins third Open Championship
2.text: Henry Cotton claimed his third Open Championship at Muirfield in 1948, with King George VI in attendance to watch the celebrated Englishman.

2.title: Henry Cotton ends American Open dominance
2.text: Henry Cotton won the 1934 Open Championship at Royal St George's, ending a decade of American victories with rounds of 67 and 65 to open the championship.

3.title: Gary Player wins first Open Championship at Muirfield
3.text: Gary Player won the 1959 Open Championship at Muirfield, the first of his three Claret Jugs and the start of his career Grand Slam, at age 23.

3.title: Major champion known for British links success
3.text: Early July 1953 belonged to Ben Hogan's only Open appearance at Carnoustie, where Scottish galleries nicknamed him the 'Wee Ice Mon' for his cool precision.

3.title: Peter Thomson wins fifth Open Championship
3.text: Australia's Peter Thomson won the 1965 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, his fifth Claret Jug, beating a field that included Arnold Palmer and Jack Nicklaus.

3.title: Ben Crenshaw's rise in the mid-1970s
3.text: By July 1976 Ben Crenshaw had emerged as one of golf's brightest young talents; he would finish runner-up at that year's Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, won by Johnny Miller.

3.title: John Henry Taylor wins fourth Open Championship
3.text: J.H. Taylor, a member of the 'Great Triumvirate,' won his fourth Open Championship at Royal Cinque Ports (Deal) in 1909.

3.title: Alf Padgham wins the Open Championship
3.text: Englishman Alf Padgham won the 1936 Open Championship at Hoylake, capping a run of strong form with a one-stroke victory.

4.title: Peter Thomson wins fourth Open at Lytham
4.text: Peter Thomson won the 1958 Open Championship at Royal Lytham & St Annes, defeating David Thomas in a 36-hole playoff for his fourth Claret Jug.

4.title: Tony Jacklin's path to glory
4.text: In the first week of July 1969, Tony Jacklin was in form ahead of his landmark Open Championship win at Royal Lytham, the first by a British golfer since 1951.

4.title: Fred Daly wins the Open Championship at Hoylake
4.text: Northern Ireland's Fred Daly won the 1947 Open Championship at Hoylake, remaining the only golfer from Northern Ireland to win the Open until Darren Clarke in 2011.

4.title: Tom Watson's dominant 1980 season
4.text: By early July 1980 Tom Watson was in the midst of a six-win PGA Tour season and would add the Open Championship at Muirfield to his haul that month.

4.title: Harry Vardon wins record sixth Open Championship
4.text: Harry Vardon won the 1914 Open Championship at Prestwick, a record sixth Claret Jug that still stands as the most in the event's history.

4.title: Henry Cotton wins second Open in foul weather
4.text: Henry Cotton won the 1937 Open Championship at Carnoustie in torrential rain, a closing 71 widely regarded as one of the finest rounds in the event's history.

5.title: Tom Watson wins his first Open Championship
5.text: Tom Watson won the 1975 Open Championship at Carnoustie, defeating Jack Newton in an 18-hole playoff for the first of his five Claret Jugs.

5.title: Heritage of the first Open Championship
5.text: The Open Championship traces to 1860 at Prestwick; by tradition, July honors the event that grew into golf's oldest major, first won by Willie Park Sr.

5.title: Bobby Locke wins fourth Open Championship
5.text: South Africa's Bobby Locke won the 1957 Open Championship at St Andrews, his fourth Claret Jug, beating Peter Thomson by three strokes.

5.title: Gary Player wins the Open at Carnoustie
5.text: Gary Player won the 1968 Open Championship at Carnoustie, holding off Jack Nicklaus and Bob Charles for his second Claret Jug.

5.title: Seve Ballesteros's breakthrough year
5.text: In July 1979 a 22-year-old Seve Ballesteros was on the verge of his first major, the Open Championship at Royal Lytham, won with his famous 'car park' shot.

5.title: Champion architect James Braid honored
5.text: James Braid, a five-time Open champion of the early 1900s, is remembered for shaping Scottish golf both as a player and as a course designer of more than 200 layouts.

6.title: Seve Ballesteros wins first Open Championship
6.text: Seve Ballesteros won the 1979 Open Championship at Royal Lytham at age 22, becoming the youngest Open champion of the 20th century and Continental Europe's first since 1907.

6.title: Arnold Palmer routs the field at Troon
6.text: Arnold Palmer won the 1962 Open Championship at Troon by six strokes, his second straight Claret Jug, popularizing the event for a new generation of Americans.

6.title: Max Faulkner wins the Open Championship
6.text: Max Faulkner won the 1951 Open Championship at Royal Portrush, the only Open held in Northern Ireland until 2019 and the last won by an Englishman until Tony Jacklin.

6.title: Tom Weiskopf's finest season
6.text: By July 1973 Tom Weiskopf was enjoying a career year that would peak with victory at the Open Championship at Troon, where he led from start to finish.

6.title: Peter Thomson wins third straight Open
6.text: Peter Thomson won the 1956 Open Championship at Hoylake, his third consecutive Claret Jug, a feat unmatched in the 20th century.

6.title: Nick Faldo dominates at St Andrews
6.text: Nick Faldo seized control of the 1990 Open Championship at St Andrews, en route to a five-stroke win and his fourth career major title.

7.title: Tom Weiskopf wins the Open Championship
7.text: Tom Weiskopf won the 1973 Open Championship at Troon wire-to-wire, equaling Arnold Palmer's tournament record total for his only major championship.

7.title: Bobby Locke wins second Open Championship
7.text: South Africa's Bobby Locke won the 1950 Open Championship at Troon, his second Claret Jug in as many years.

7.title: Roberto De Vicenzo wins the Open at Hoylake
7.text: Argentina's Roberto De Vicenzo won the 1967 Open Championship at Hoylake at age 44, a hugely popular victory after years of near-misses.

7.title: Peter Thomson wins second Open Championship
7.text: Peter Thomson won the 1955 Open Championship at St Andrews, the second of his five Claret Jugs, by two strokes over Johnny Fallon.

7.title: Sandy Lyle ends the British drought
7.text: In July 1985 Sandy Lyle was poised to become the first British Open champion in 16 years, which he achieved at Royal St George's later that month.

7.title: Lee Trevino defends his Open title at Muirfield
7.text: Lee Trevino retained the Open Championship at Muirfield in 1972, chipping in repeatedly down the stretch to deny Jack Nicklaus the Grand Slam and beat Tony Jacklin.

8.title: Watson and Nicklaus begin the Duel in the Sun
8.text: At Turnberry in 1977, Tom Watson and Jack Nicklaus separated from the field, setting up the legendary final-day 'Duel in the Sun' that would decide the Open Championship.

8.title: Kel Nagle wins Centenary Open at St Andrews
8.text: Australia's Kel Nagle won the 1960 Centenary Open Championship at St Andrews, edging Arnold Palmer by one stroke in Palmer's first Open appearance.

8.title: Jack Nicklaus completes the career Grand Slam
8.text: Jack Nicklaus won the 1966 Open Championship at Muirfield, completing the career Grand Slam at age 26 with a one-stroke win over Doug Sanders and Dave Thomas.

8.title: Peter Thomson wins his first Open Championship
8.text: Peter Thomson won the 1954 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, the first of his five Claret Jugs, launching one of the great Open careers.

8.title: Tom Watson's run at Royal Birkdale
8.text: By early July 1983 Tom Watson was chasing his fifth Open title, which he would win at Royal Birkdale, his last and a record-tying fifth Claret Jug of the modern era.

8.title: Dick Burton wins the last pre-war Open
8.text: Englishman Dick Burton won the 1939 Open Championship at St Andrews, the final Open played before World War II suspended the championship for six years.

9.title: Tom Watson wins the Duel in the Sun at Turnberry
9.text: Tom Watson edged Jack Nicklaus by one stroke at Turnberry to win the 1977 Open Championship, the climax of the 'Duel in the Sun' after both shot 65 and 66 to finish 10 clear of third.

9.title: Tony Lema wins the Open at St Andrews
9.text: 'Champagne' Tony Lema won the 1964 Open Championship at St Andrews by five strokes over Jack Nicklaus, despite only one practice round on the Old Course.

9.title: Lee Trevino wins the Open at Royal Birkdale
9.text: Lee Trevino won the 1971 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, completing a remarkable summer in which he won the U.S., Canadian and British Opens within a month.

9.title: Young Tom Morris's record reign begins
9.text: In the late 1860s Young Tom Morris dominated the Open Championship at Prestwick, winning three in a row from 1868 to 1870 to claim the Championship Belt outright.

9.title: Bobby Locke wins his first Open Championship
9.text: South Africa's Bobby Locke won the 1949 Open Championship at Royal St George's, beating Harry Bradshaw in a 36-hole playoff for his first Claret Jug.

9.title: Bill Rogers's breakout summer
9.text: By July 1981 American Bill Rogers was enjoying a career season and would win the Open Championship at Royal St George's later that month, his only major.

10.title: Johnny Miller wins the Open at Royal Birkdale
10.text: Johnny Miller won the 1976 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale by six strokes, holding off a 19-year-old Seve Ballesteros and Jack Nicklaus.

10.title: Tony Jacklin wins the Open Championship
10.text: Tony Jacklin won the 1969 Open Championship at Royal Lytham, becoming the first British golfer to win the event since Max Faulkner in 1951.

10.title: Bill Rogers wins the Open at Sandwich
10.text: American Bill Rogers won the 1981 Open Championship at Royal St George's by four strokes over Bernhard Langer for his lone major title.

10.title: Nick Faldo's 18 pars at Muirfield
10.text: By mid-July 1987 Nick Faldo was set for his first major, the Open at Muirfield, where he famously made 18 straight pars in the final round to win.

10.title: Three-time Open winner Gary Player's debut title
10.text: Gary Player's first Open Championship in 1959 at Muirfield launched a Hall of Fame major career that would include nine professional majors.

10.title: Roberto De Vicenzo, prolific worldwide winner
10.text: Argentina's Roberto De Vicenzo, who won more than 230 tournaments worldwide and the 1967 Open Championship, was among the era's most traveled professionals.

11.title: Greg Norman wins his first Open Championship
11.text: Greg Norman led the 1986 Open Championship at Turnberry, which he would win for his first major after a brilliant second-round 63 in brutal conditions.

11.title: Bob Charles wins the Open Championship
11.text: New Zealand's Bob Charles won the 1963 Open Championship at Royal Lytham, defeating Phil Rodgers in a playoff to become the first left-hander to win a major.

11.title: Gary Player wins third Open Championship
11.text: Gary Player won the 1974 Open Championship at Royal Lytham by four strokes, his third and final Claret Jug.

11.title: Tom Watson wins third Open Championship
11.text: Tom Watson won the 1980 Open Championship at Muirfield by four strokes over Lee Trevino for his third Claret Jug.

11.title: Bobby Jones and the famous Lytham bunker shot
11.text: Bobby Jones's 1926 Open win at Royal Lytham featured a heroic blind mashie-iron shot from sandy ground on the 17th, commemorated by a plaque on the course.

11.title: Ian Baker-Finch's Open form
11.text: By mid-July 1991 Australia's Ian Baker-Finch was rounding into the form that would carry him to the Open Championship at Royal Birkdale that summer.

12.title: Nick Faldo wins his first Open Championship
12.text: Nick Faldo won the 1987 Open Championship at Muirfield, closing with 18 consecutive pars to overtake Paul Azinger for the first of his three Claret Jugs.

12.title: Bobby Locke's St Andrews controversy
12.text: Bobby Locke won the 1957 Open at St Andrews despite marking his ball incorrectly on the final green, with officials ruling his win stood for the good of the game.

12.title: Major champion and Ryder Cup figure honored
12.text: Mid-July has long centered on the Open Championship, with stars like Bernhard Langer, the 1981 runner-up at Sandwich, building Hall of Fame résumés.

12.title: Seve Ballesteros, the swashbuckling Spaniard
12.text: Seve Ballesteros, who electrified the 1979 Open at Lytham, became Europe's defining golfer of the 1980s with five major titles.

12.title: Nick Faldo runs away at Muirfield
12.text: Nick Faldo was building a commanding lead at the 1992 Open Championship at Muirfield, which he would win for his third Claret Jug after a tense finish.

12.title: Jack Nicklaus closes in on St Andrews title
12.text: By mid-July 1970 Jack Nicklaus was contending at St Andrews, where he would beat Doug Sanders in a playoff after Sanders missed a short putt to win.

13.title: Nick Faldo wins second straight major
13.text: Nick Faldo took command of the 1990 Open Championship at St Andrews, where he would win by five strokes to add the Claret Jug to that year's Masters title.

13.title: Bobby Jones wins the Open during his Grand Slam
13.text: Bobby Jones won the 1930 Open Championship at Hoylake, the second leg of his unique single-season Grand Slam of all four major amateur and open titles.

13.title: Arnold Palmer's record Troon win
13.text: Arnold Palmer's six-shot win at Troon in 1962 set a then-record total and cemented the Open's revival as a championship Americans coveted.

13.title: Peter Thomson's playoff at Lytham
13.text: Peter Thomson won his fourth Open in a 1958 playoff at Royal Lytham, underscoring his mastery of links golf in the 1950s.

13.title: Ben Curtis stuns the field at Royal St George's
13.text: In July 2003 unheralded American Ben Curtis arrived at Royal St George's ranked 396th in the world, then shocked golf by winning the Open in his major debut.

13.title: Fred Daly's historic Open win
13.text: Fred Daly's 1947 Open victory at Hoylake remains a landmark for Irish golf and stood as Northern Ireland's only Open title for 64 years.

14.title: Major champion Justin Leonard's Open form
14.text: Justin Leonard, the 1997 Open champion at Royal Troon, ranked among the steadiest links players of his era during the early 2000s seasons.

14.title: John Daly contends at St Andrews
14.text: In mid-July 1995 John Daly was in the mix at the Open Championship at St Andrews, which he would win in a four-hole playoff over Costantino Rocca.

14.title: Roberto De Vicenzo's beloved Open win
14.text: Roberto De Vicenzo's popular 1967 Open victory at Hoylake came at age 44, ending years of major heartbreak for the Argentine star.

14.title: Stenson and Mickelson set the stage at Troon
14.text: The 2016 Open Championship opened at Royal Troon, where Henrik Stenson and Phil Mickelson would stage a final-day duel rivaling 1977's classic.

14.title: The 150th Open begins at St Andrews
14.text: The milestone 150th Open Championship teed off at the Old Course at St Andrews in July 2022, the spiritual home of golf, won days later by Cameron Smith.

14.title: Sandy Lyle ends the home drought
14.text: Sandy Lyle's 1985 Open win at Royal St George's, the first by a British golfer in 16 years, sparked a wave of European major success.

15.title: Jack Nicklaus completes career Grand Slam at Muirfield
15.text: Jack Nicklaus won the 1966 Open Championship at Muirfield in mid-July to complete the career Grand Slam, becoming at the time the youngest to do so.

15.title: Peter Thomson's St Andrews mastery
15.text: Peter Thomson's 1955 Open win at St Andrews showed his command of the Old Course, where his canny links play was perfectly suited.

15.title: Carnoustie's brutal test begins
15.text: The 1999 Open Championship began at a fearsome Carnoustie setup, where high scores set the stage for Jean van de Velde's collapse and Paul Lawrie's comeback.

15.title: Lee Trevino chips in to deny Nicklaus
15.text: Lee Trevino's run of chip-ins at Muirfield in 1972 helped him retain the Open and famously block Jack Nicklaus's bid for the calendar Grand Slam.

15.title: Padraig Harrington's first major nears
15.text: By mid-July 2007 Padraig Harrington was set for his breakthrough Open Championship at Carnoustie, won in a playoff over Sergio Garcia.

15.title: Johnny Miller's runaway at Birkdale
15.text: Johnny Miller's six-stroke win at Royal Birkdale in 1976 capped a dominant Open performance, with a teenage Seve Ballesteros announcing himself in second.

16.title: Jean van de Velde's lead at Carnoustie
16.text: Heading into the weekend of the 1999 Open at Carnoustie, Jean van de Velde held a commanding position before his infamous final-hole triple bogey.

16.title: Tom Watson nearly wins at age 59
16.text: During the 2009 Open at Turnberry, 59-year-old Tom Watson stood on the brink of an astonishing sixth Claret Jug before a final-hole bogey forced a playoff.

16.title: John Daly's St Andrews triumph nears
16.text: By mid-July 1995 John Daly was contending at the Open at St Andrews, which he won in a playoff over Costantino Rocca for his second major.

16.title: Tiger Woods's masterclass at Hoylake
16.text: In July 2006 Tiger Woods was charting a precise, driver-free route around a fiery Royal Liverpool, winning the Open weeks after his father's death.

16.title: Peter Thomson's fifth and final Open title
16.text: Peter Thomson won his fifth Open Championship at Royal Birkdale in 1965, joining J.H. Taylor, James Braid and Tom Watson among the event's most prolific champions.

16.title: Collin Morikawa's Open debut nears
16.text: At Royal St George's in July 2021, Open Championship debutant Collin Morikawa was poised to claim the Claret Jug in his very first attempt.

17.title: Henrik Stenson wins the Open in record fashion
17.text: Henrik Stenson shot a final-round 63 at Royal Troon to win the 2016 Open Championship at 20-under 264, a major scoring record, beating Phil Mickelson by three.

17.title: Cameron Smith wins the 150th Open at St Andrews
17.text: Cameron Smith closed with a 64 to win the 150th Open Championship at St Andrews at 20-under, coming from behind to beat Cameron Young and Rory McIlroy.

17.title: Nick Price wins the Open at Turnberry
17.text: Zimbabwe's Nick Price won the 1994 Open Championship at Turnberry, holing a long eagle putt on the 71st hole to overtake Jesper Parnevik.

17.title: Seve Ballesteros wins third Open Championship
17.text: Seve Ballesteros won the 1988 Open Championship at Royal Lytham, closing with a 65 to beat Nick Price for his third and final Claret Jug.

17.title: Tom Watson wins fifth Open Championship
17.text: Tom Watson won the 1983 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale by one stroke, his fifth and final Claret Jug, tying the modern record.

17.title: Darren Clarke wins the Open at Sandwich
17.text: Northern Ireland's Darren Clarke won the 2011 Open Championship at Royal St George's at age 42, a hugely popular first major after two decades of trying.

18.title: Paul Lawrie wins the Open after Van de Velde collapse
18.text: Paul Lawrie won the 1999 Open at Carnoustie in a four-hole playoff over Jean van de Velde and Justin Leonard, completing the largest final-round comeback in major history.

18.title: Collin Morikawa wins the Open on debut
18.text: Collin Morikawa won the 2021 Open Championship at Royal St George's in his debut, closing with a 66 to beat Jordan Spieth and become the first to win two different majors at the first attempt.

18.title: Nick Faldo born in Welwyn Garden City
18.text: Six-time major champion Sir Nick Faldo, winner of three Open Championships and three Masters and a Ryder Cup stalwart, was born on this day in England.

18.title: Greg Norman wins second Open with record total
18.text: Greg Norman won the 1993 Open Championship at Royal St George's with a then-record 13-under 267, closing with a 64 to beat Nick Faldo and Bernhard Langer.

18.title: Peter Thomson's links legacy
18.text: Peter Thomson, whose final Open win came in July 1965, remains one of only a handful of golfers to win the Open Championship five times.

18.title: Seve Ballesteros announces himself at Birkdale
18.text: A 19-year-old Seve Ballesteros tied for second at the 1976 Open at Royal Birkdale, a star-making performance that launched a legendary career.

19.title: Stewart Cink beats Tom Watson in Open playoff
19.text: Stewart Cink defeated 59-year-old Tom Watson in a four-hole playoff at Turnberry to win the 2009 Open Championship, denying Watson a fairy-tale sixth Claret Jug.

19.title: Nick Faldo wins third Open Championship
19.text: Nick Faldo won the 1992 Open Championship at Muirfield, recovering from a late wobble to beat John Cook by one for his fifth career major.

19.title: Bill Rogers's Sandwich victory
19.text: Bill Rogers's commanding 1981 Open win at Royal St George's came during his best season, when he was named PGA Tour Player of the Year.

19.title: Nick Faldo's relentless Muirfield finish
19.text: Nick Faldo's 18 straight pars in the final round of the 1987 Open at Muirfield delivered his first major and defined his methodical brilliance.

19.title: Mark O'Meara wins the Open at Royal Birkdale
19.text: Mark O'Meara won the 1998 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale in a playoff over Brian Watts, adding the Claret Jug to the Masters he won that spring.

19.title: Harry Vardon wins his first Open Championship
19.text: Harry Vardon won the first of his record six Open Championships in 1896 at Muirfield, beating J.H. Taylor in a 36-hole playoff.

20.title: Rory McIlroy wins the Open at Hoylake
20.text: Rory McIlroy won the 2014 Open Championship at Royal Liverpool wire-to-wire, finishing two clear of Sergio Garcia and Rickie Fowler for his third major title.

20.title: Padraig Harrington defends his Open title
20.text: Padraig Harrington retained the Open Championship at Royal Birkdale in 2008, beating Ian Poulter by four to become the first European to win back-to-back Opens since 1906.

20.title: Ben Curtis wins the Open in his major debut
20.text: Ben Curtis, ranked 396th in the world, won the 2003 Open Championship at Royal St George's in his first major appearance, one of golf's great upsets.

20.title: Justin Leonard wins the Open at Troon
20.text: Justin Leonard rallied from five strokes back with a closing 65 to win the 1997 Open Championship at Royal Troon, overtaking Jesper Parnevik.

20.title: Greg Norman wins his first Open Championship
20.text: Greg Norman won the 1986 Open Championship at Turnberry, his first major, after a brilliant second-round 63 in the wind helped him pull clear of the field.

20.title: Tom Watson's first major arrives
20.text: Tom Watson's 1975 playoff win at Carnoustie launched a major career that would yield eight titles, including five Open Championships.

21.title: Ernie Els wins the Open in a playoff at Muirfield
21.text: Ernie Els won the 2002 Open Championship at Muirfield, prevailing in a four-hole playoff and then sudden death over Thomas Levet for his third major.

21.title: Phil Mickelson wins the Open at Muirfield
21.text: Phil Mickelson closed with a brilliant 66 to win the 2013 Open Championship at Muirfield by three strokes, completing a career mastery of links golf.

21.title: Shane Lowry wins the Open at Royal Portrush
21.text: Ireland's Shane Lowry won the 2019 Open Championship at Royal Portrush by six strokes, the first Open in Northern Ireland since 1951, before delirious home crowds.

21.title: Xander Schauffele wins the Open at Royal Troon
21.text: Xander Schauffele closed with a 65 to win the 2024 Open Championship at Royal Troon by two strokes, his second major title of the year after the PGA Championship.

21.title: Gary Player's Carnoustie crown
21.text: Gary Player's 1968 Open win at Carnoustie came over Jack Nicklaus and Bob Charles, part of a major haul that spanned three decades.

21.title: Ian Baker-Finch wins the Open at Birkdale
21.text: Australia's Ian Baker-Finch won the 1991 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, surging to the front with a brilliant outward 29 in the final round.

22.title: Francesco Molinari wins the Open at Carnoustie
22.text: Francesco Molinari posted a bogey-free closing 69 to win the 2018 Open Championship at Carnoustie, becoming the first Italian to win a major.

22.title: Ernie Els wins fourth major at Royal Lytham
22.text: Ernie Els won the 2012 Open Championship at Royal Lytham by one stroke after Adam Scott bogeyed his last four holes, claiming a second Claret Jug.

22.title: Padraig Harrington wins his first Open Championship
22.text: Padraig Harrington won the 2007 Open Championship at Carnoustie in a playoff over Sergio Garcia, the first Irishman to win the Open since Fred Daly in 1947.

22.title: David Duval wins the Open at Royal Lytham
22.text: David Duval won the 2001 Open Championship at Royal Lytham by three strokes for his only major title, a closing 67 sealing the Claret Jug.

22.title: Nick Faldo wins second Open at St Andrews
22.text: Nick Faldo won the 1990 Open Championship at St Andrews by five strokes over Mark McNulty and Payne Stewart for his fourth career major.

22.title: Branden Grace's record-setting day at Birkdale
22.text: During the 2017 Open at Royal Birkdale, Branden Grace shot 62 in the third round, the first sub-63 round in men's major championship history.

23.title: Tiger Woods wins the Open and completes the Grand Slam
23.text: Tiger Woods won the 2000 Open Championship at St Andrews by eight strokes at 19-under, a major record, becoming the youngest to complete the career Grand Slam at 24.

23.title: Jordan Spieth wins the Open at Royal Birkdale
23.text: Jordan Spieth won the 2017 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, recovering from a wild bogey on the 13th with a closing flurry to beat Matt Kuchar by three.

23.title: Brian Harman wins the Open at Royal Liverpool
23.text: Brian Harman won the 2023 Open Championship at Royal Liverpool by six strokes in rainy conditions, his first major title at age 36.

23.title: John Daly wins the Open at St Andrews
23.text: John Daly won the 1995 Open Championship at St Andrews in a four-hole playoff over Costantino Rocca, who had forced extra holes with a long birdie from the Valley of Sin.

23.title: Mark Calcavecchia wins the Open at Troon
23.text: Mark Calcavecchia won the 1989 Open Championship at Royal Troon in the first four-hole aggregate playoff, beating Greg Norman and Wayne Grady.

23.title: Jack Nicklaus wins third Open at St Andrews
23.text: Jack Nicklaus won the 1978 Open Championship at St Andrews for the third of his Claret Jugs, fittingly capturing the title on the Old Course.

24.title: Nick Price wins the Open Championship
24.text: Nick Price won the 1994 Open Championship at Turnberry, capping a dominant stretch in which he also won the PGA Championship a month later to reach World No. 1.

24.title: Tiger Woods wins second Open at St Andrews
24.text: Tiger Woods won the 2005 Open Championship at St Andrews by five strokes, his second Claret Jug at the Old Course and 10th career major.

24.title: Seve Ballesteros's masterful 65 at Lytham
24.text: Seve Ballesteros's closing 65 to win the 1988 Open at Royal Lytham is regarded as one of the greatest final rounds in Open history.

24.title: The legacy of the Duel in the Sun
24.text: The 1977 Open at Turnberry, decided weeks earlier by Tom Watson over Jack Nicklaus, endures as the standard against which great final-day duels are measured.

24.title: Jack White's record Open at Sandwich
24.text: Jack White's 1904 Open win at Royal St George's, the first sub-300 total in the event, marked a turning point as scoring standards rose in championship golf.

24.title: Greg Norman's record Sandwich charge
24.text: Greg Norman's closing 64 to win the 1993 Open at Royal St George's set a record aggregate and is remembered among the finest rounds to win a major.

25.title: Bobby Jones, the great amateur champion
25.text: Bobby Jones, who won the Open Championship three times in the 1920s, completed his unmatched single-season Grand Slam in 1930 before retiring at 28.

25.title: Todd Hamilton wins the Open at Troon
25.text: Journeyman Todd Hamilton won the 2004 Open Championship at Royal Troon in a four-hole playoff over Ernie Els, using a hybrid to chip his way to victory.

25.title: Lee Trevino's triple-Open summer
25.text: Lee Trevino's 1971 Open win at Royal Birkdale completed a run of three national Opens in a month, earning him Sports Illustrated Sportsman of the Year.

25.title: Paul Lawrie's record comeback at Carnoustie
25.text: Paul Lawrie's 1999 Open win came after he trailed by 10 strokes entering the final round, still the largest final-round deficit overcome in major history.

25.title: Louis Oosthuizen wins the Open at St Andrews
25.text: South Africa's Louis Oosthuizen won the 2010 Open Championship at St Andrews by seven strokes, a breakthrough major won wire-to-wire.

25.title: Tom Watson's Open and U.S. Open double looms
25.text: By late July 1982 Tom Watson had already won that year's U.S. Open at Pebble Beach and would add the Open Championship at Troon to complete a rare double.

26.title: Tom Watson wins fourth Open Championship
26.text: Tom Watson won the 1982 Open Championship at Royal Troon, weeks after his iconic U.S. Open chip-in at Pebble Beach, completing the U.S. Open–Open double.

26.title: Nick Faldo's Muirfield mastery
26.text: Nick Faldo's 1992 Open win at Muirfield was his second on the course, where his ball-striking precision suited the demanding links perfectly.

26.title: Three-time Open champion Nick Faldo
26.text: Nick Faldo, born in July 1957, claimed Open Championships in 1987, 1990 and 1992 to become one of the finest links players of his generation.

26.title: Ben Hogan's perfect 1953 season
26.text: Ben Hogan's only Open Championship, at Carnoustie in 1953, completed a season in which he won the Masters, U.S. Open and Open in his three major starts.

26.title: Gary Player's first major
26.text: Gary Player's 1959 Open win at Muirfield was the first major for the South African who would go on to win nine and complete the career Grand Slam.

26.title: Harry Vardon's six-Open legacy
26.text: Harry Vardon, whose first Open came in 1896, would win a record six Claret Jugs, a mark that still stands more than a century later.

27.title: Jordan Spieth born in Dallas, Texas
27.text: Three-time major champion Jordan Spieth, winner of the 2015 Masters and U.S. Open and the 2017 Open Championship, was born on this day in Dallas.

27.title: Tom Watson wins third Open at Muirfield
27.text: Tom Watson won the 1980 Open Championship at Muirfield by four strokes over Lee Trevino, one of three majors he claimed that overlapped this part of July.

27.title: Peter Thomson's links genius
27.text: Peter Thomson, whose fourth Open came in July 1958, was renowned for a simple, controlled swing ideally suited to the firm, fast conditions of British links.

27.title: Tom Watson's major breakthrough
27.text: Tom Watson's first major, the 1975 Open at Carnoustie, began a rivalry with Jack Nicklaus that produced some of the sport's most memorable moments.

27.title: Ben Curtis's stunning Open title
27.text: Ben Curtis's 2003 win at Royal St George's, in his first major and ranked outside the world's top 300, remains one of golf's most improbable championships.

27.title: Bobby Jones marches toward the Grand Slam
27.text: Bobby Jones's 1930 Open win at Hoylake was the second leg of his Impregnable Quadrilateral, the only single-season sweep of golf's four major championships.

28.title: Sandy Lyle wins the Open at Royal St George's
28.text: Scotland's Sandy Lyle won the 1985 Open Championship at Royal St George's, the first British winner since Tony Jacklin in 1969.

28.title: Tom Lehman wins the Open at Royal Lytham
28.text: Tom Lehman won the 1996 Open Championship at Royal Lytham by two strokes, the first American club professional product to win the Claret Jug in his era.

28.title: Ian Baker-Finch's brilliant final round
28.text: Ian Baker-Finch's outward 29 in the final round of the 1991 Open at Royal Birkdale carried him to a runaway victory and his only major.

28.title: Ernie Els survives Muirfield
28.text: Ernie Els's 2002 Open win at Muirfield came after a Saturday storm scattered the leaders, with Els outlasting a four-man playoff for his third major.

28.title: Gary Player's three-decade major span
28.text: Gary Player, the 1968 Open champion at Carnoustie, won majors in the 1950s, 1960s, 1970s and 1980s era of senior golf, a testament to his longevity.

28.title: Lee Trevino denies Nicklaus the Slam
28.text: Lee Trevino's 1972 Open win at Muirfield ended Jack Nicklaus's bid for the calendar-year Grand Slam after Nicklaus had won the Masters and U.S. Open.

29.title: Seve Ballesteros wins the Open at St Andrews
29.text: Seve Ballesteros won the 1984 Open Championship at St Andrews, his birdie and celebratory fist-pump on the 18th green becoming one of golf's iconic images.

29.title: David Duval's lone major triumph
29.text: David Duval's 2001 Open win at Royal Lytham was the only major of a career that also saw him reach World No. 1 and shoot a final-round 59 on the PGA Tour.

29.title: Seve Ballesteros's car-park Open
29.text: Seve Ballesteros's 1979 Open win at Royal Lytham included a wild drive into a car park on the 16th that led to a birdie, embodying his daring genius.

29.title: Nick Faldo's two-major year
29.text: Nick Faldo's 1990 Open win at St Andrews, paired with that year's Masters, made him the dominant player in the world and a back-to-back Augusta champion.

29.title: Francesco Molinari's historic Italian first
29.text: Francesco Molinari's 2018 Open win at Carnoustie made him the first Italian major champion and anchored an undefeated Ryder Cup that autumn.

29.title: Jack Nicklaus joins the Grand Slam club
29.text: Jack Nicklaus's 1966 Open win at Muirfield made him the fourth golfer to complete the career Grand Slam, a club he would later define by adding to his major total.

30.title: Justin Rose born in Johannesburg
30.text: Justin Rose, the 2013 U.S. Open champion and 2016 Olympic gold medalist who reached World No. 1, was born on this day in South Africa before representing England.

30.title: Mark Calcavecchia's playoff Open
30.text: Mark Calcavecchia's 1989 Open win at Royal Troon came in the championship's first four-hole aggregate playoff, beating Greg Norman and Wayne Grady.

30.title: Tiger Woods's record St Andrews romp
30.text: Tiger Woods's 2000 Open win at St Andrews, by eight at 19-under, made him the youngest career Grand Slam winner and confirmed his historic dominance.

30.title: John Daly's improbable Open
30.text: John Daly's 1995 Open win at St Andrews, his second major after the 1991 PGA, came via a playoff over Costantino Rocca on golf's most famous links.

30.title: Tiger Woods's emotional Hoylake win
30.text: Tiger Woods's 2006 Open win at Royal Liverpool, achieved by strategically avoiding driver on the firm course, came shortly after the death of his father Earl.

30.title: Louis Oosthuizen's St Andrews breakthrough
30.text: Louis Oosthuizen's seven-shot 2010 Open win at St Andrews was a surprise runaway that announced him as a major force in world golf.

31.title: Nick Price's banner summer
31.text: Nick Price's 1994 Open win at Turnberry was followed weeks later by the PGA Championship, a two-major run that lifted him to World No. 1.

31.title: Tiger Woods's second St Andrews title
31.text: Tiger Woods's 2005 Open win at St Andrews by five strokes was his 10th major, reaffirming his command of the Old Course where he never made a bogey on a bunker.

31.title: Bill Rogers's Player of the Year season
31.text: Bill Rogers's 1981 Open win at Royal St George's was the centerpiece of a season in which he won worldwide and was the PGA Tour Player of the Year.

31.title: Tom Lehman's Lytham triumph
31.text: Tom Lehman's 1996 Open win at Royal Lytham preceded a year in which he topped the PGA Tour money list and was named Player of the Year.

31.title: Nick Faldo's first major at Muirfield
31.text: Nick Faldo's 1987 Open win, sealed by 18 final-round pars at Muirfield, was the first of six majors for the most successful British golfer of his era.

31.title: Tony Lema's St Andrews masterpiece
31.text: Tony Lema's 1964 Open win at St Andrews, achieved with minimal practice on the Old Course, was a five-stroke triumph over Jack Nicklaus before his tragic early death.
